topics.title: Spring Boot — Auto-Configuration, Profiles & Starters
topics.description: Spring Boot eliminates boilerplate configuration by applying sensible defaults automatically. @SpringBootApplication triggers component scan + auto-configuration. Starters (spring-boot-starter-web, starter-data-jpa) bundle compatible dependency sets. application.properties/yml controls everything: server port, datasource, logging. Profiles (dev/staging/prod) activate different property sets and beans for different environments.
topics.story: Building a Spring app pre-Boot was like assembling IKEA furniture with 200 parts and no instruction manual — you had to know exactly which XML files, beans, and library versions to configure together. Spring Boot is like buying pre-assembled furniture: out-of-the-box it works for most cases. Want a web server? Add starter-web and Boot auto-configures Tomcat, Jackson, DispatcherServlet. Need a database? Add starter-data-jpa and a JDBC driver — Boot auto-creates DataSource, EntityManager, TransactionManager.
topics.analogy: @SpringBootApplication = turning on a smartphone (auto-configures everything). starter-web = installing the messaging app bundle (brings all required components). application.yml = phone settings menu. @Profile('prod') = switching to Work Mode (different settings, different apps visible). auto-configuration = phone detecting your SIM and configuring carrier settings automatically. @ConditionalOnMissingBean = 'configure this only if user hasn't set it up manually'.
topics.memoryAnchor: ABC of @SpringBootApplication — @Configuration (A=Application config beans) + @ComponentScan (B=Bean hunting in current package tree) + @EnableAutoConfiguration (C=Convention over configuration magic). Auto-config BOUNCER rule: @ConditionalOnClass = only activate if this JAR is on classpath. @ConditionalOnMissingBean = only activate if YOU have not defined your own. Profiles mnemonic: DEEP — Dev / Error(test) / External(prod) / Properties. @Value injects a single value. @ConfigurationProperties injects an entire prefix group as a typed object. Starters = pre-bundled dependency sets. spring-boot-starter-web includes Tomcat + Jackson + Spring MVC in one line.
topics.firstPrinciples: Auto-configuration works via @Conditional annotations — Spring Boot only configures components when specific classes are on the classpath and specific beans are absent. This is 'convention over configuration': if DataSource driver jar is present and no DataSource bean is defined, Boot creates one from application.properties. You can always override any auto-configured bean by defining your own @Bean.
topics.bruteForce: Pre-Boot Spring MVC: web.xml → DispatcherServlet mapping. applicationContext.xml → DataSource bean, TransactionManager bean, SessionFactory bean, ViewResolver bean. persistence.xml for JPA. pom.xml with 20+ carefully versioned dependencies. A week of configuration before writing business logic.
topics.optimizedApproach: Spring Boot: add spring-boot-starter-web + spring-boot-starter-data-jpa to pom.xml. Add application.properties: spring.datasource.url=... Done. The entire web+jpa stack is auto-configured. Write your @RestController and @Entity — no XML, no manual beans. Customise only what defaults don't cover.
topics.whenToUse: application.properties: simple key-value config. application.yml: nested structures, more readable. @ConfigurationProperties: binding a whole prefix to a POJO (e.g., app.mail.host → MailProperties). @Value: injecting a single property. Profiles: never put prod credentials in code — use environment variable override or secrets manager. Actuator: always enable /health in production behind auth.

examples.title: application.yml, Profiles & @ConfigurationProperties
examples.code:
# application.yml (base)
server:
  port: 8080
  error:
    include-message: always

spring:
  application:
    name: devlearner
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:h2:mem:testdb
    driver-class-name: org.h2.Driver
  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: update
    show-sql: false

app:
  mail:
    host: smtp.example.com
    port: 587
    from: noreply@example.com
  features:
    review-enabled: true
    max-upload-mb: 10

---
# application-dev.yml (activated by spring.profiles.active=dev)
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: dev
  jpa:
    show-sql: true  # override
  h2:
    console:
      enabled: true

logging:
  level:
    com.learnsystem: DEBUG

---
# application-prod.yml
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: prod
  datasource:
    url: ${DB_URL}       # from environment variable
    username: ${DB_USER}
    password: ${DB_PASS}
  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: validate # never create/drop in prod

logging:
  level:
    root: WARN
examples.explanation: Multi-document YAML (---) defines profile-specific overrides in one file. Properties lower in the file override higher ones when a profile is active. ${ENV_VAR} reads from environment — prod credentials never in source code. @ConfigurationProperties(prefix='app.mail') binds the app.mail.* properties to a MailProperties POJO automatically.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner application.properties: spring.datasource.url, spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update, jwt.secret, app.cors.origin. Never hardcode passwords — use ${DB_PASSWORD} from environment. Kubernetes: ConfigMap → application.properties mounted at runtime. AWS: Secrets Manager → environment variables.

examples.title: @ConfigurationProperties + Custom Auto-Configuration
examples.explanation: @ConfigurationProperties provides type-safe configuration binding with validation support (@Validated + @NotNull). @ConditionalOnProperty only creates beans when a property exists — good for feature flags. @ConditionalOnMissingBean lets library users override defaults. This is exactly how Spring Boot's own auto-configuration works (DataSourceAutoConfiguration, WebMvcAutoConfiguration).
examples.realWorldUse: Your DevLearner app can bind jwt.secret, jwt.expiry, cors.allowed-origins to a SecurityProperties class. Stripe/payment integration: @ConditionalOnProperty('app.stripe.enabled'). Feature flags: @ConditionalOnProperty('features.new-dashboard.enabled'). Multi-tenant config: separate @ConfigurationProperties per tenant.

problems.title: Property Binding with @ConfigurationProperties
problems.hint2: Fields: maxConnections, timeoutSeconds, host — Spring handles camelCase to kebab-case mapping automatically.
problems.sampleOutput: 400 Bad Request: {"name": "must not be blank", "email": "must be a valid email"}

problems.title: Profile-Based Bean Selection
problems.description:
Configure Spring Boot application with profiles (dev/prod).

**application.yml:**
```yaml
spring:
  profiles:
    active: dev
```

**application-dev.yml:**
```yaml
server.port: 8080
logging.level.root: DEBUG
spring.datasource.url: jdbc:h2:mem:testdb
```

**application-prod.yml:**
```yaml
server.port: 80
logging.level.root: WARN
spring.datasource.url: ${DATABASE_URL}
```

**Run with:** `java -jar app.jar --spring.profiles.active=prod`

**Key insight:** Profile-specific files override base `application.yml`. Env vars override everything — ideal for 12-factor app config.
problems.hint1: @Bean @Profile('dev') public String devDataSource() returns H2 URL.
problems.hint2: @Bean @Profile('prod') public String prodDataSource() returns MySQL URL.
problems.hint3: Activate with System.setProperty('spring.profiles.active', 'dev') before context start, or -Dspring.profiles.active=dev.
problems.sampleOutput: App starts on port 80, uses ${DATABASE_URL}, WARN-level logging

problems.title: Externalized Config Precedence
problems.hint1: Precedence (highest first): command line args > ENV vars > profile properties > application.properties.
problems.hint2: ENV var APP_GREETING overrides application-dev.properties app.greeting.
problems.hint3: Demonstrate with @Value('${app.greeting}') and print the value.
problems.testCases: 1. Command line > 2. ENV vars > 3. Profile properties > 4. Base properties

problems.title: Actuator Health Indicator
problems.description:
Build a custom Actuator health indicator that checks whether an external API is reachable, and aggregate it with a simulated disk-space check.

**Actuator health endpoint** auto-discovers all `HealthIndicator` beans. When any returns `Health.down()`, the overall status becomes DOWN — load balancers and Kubernetes liveness probes use this to detect failures.

**Key insight:** Real production apps check external dependencies (third-party APIs, caches) as custom indicators. Return structured details so on-call engineers know exactly what failed without opening logs.
problems.hint1: Implement HealthIndicator interface and override health() method.
problems.hint2: Use Health.up().withDetail(key, value).build() for healthy and Health.down().withDetail(...).build() for unhealthy.
problems.hint3: Aggregate multiple indicators: if any check fails, return DOWN status with details listing which checks failed.
problems.sampleInput: Two API indicators (one https, one http) + disk check with insufficient space

problems.title: Spring Boot Startup Lifecycle
problems.description:
Demonstrate the exact order of Spring Boot initialization hooks: `@PostConstruct`, `InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()`, `ApplicationRunner`, and `CommandLineRunner`. These hooks let you run initialization logic at specific stages of startup.

**Use cases:** `@PostConstruct` — validate config fields; `ApplicationRunner` — warm up caches after full context is ready; `CommandLineRunner` — print startup summary or run DB migrations.

**Key insight:** Understanding lifecycle order is critical for DevLearner's `SeedDataRunner` — it must run AFTER `DataInitializer` and `CurriculumSeeder`. The `@Order` annotation controls which runner fires first.
problems.hint2: CommandLineRunner receives String[] args; ApplicationRunner receives ApplicationArguments.
problems.hint3: @Order(1) runs before @Order(2). Implement three classes and observe the print order.
problems.testCases:
1. Constructor called: config=dev-config
2. @PostConstruct: validate config fields, set defaults
3. InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(): additional wiring
4. ApplicationRunner @Order(1): warm up cache
5. ApplicationRunner @Order(2): seed database if empty
Application fully started
problems.sampleOutput: Five lifecycle steps printed in correct order

problems.title: Custom Auto-Configuration with @Conditional
problems.description:
Simulate Spring Boot's `@ConditionalOnProperty` and `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` auto-configuration logic. Auto-configuration should only create a bean when no user-defined bean of the same type exists and a required property is set.

**Real-world example:** Spring Boot's `DataSourceAutoConfiguration` only creates a `DataSource` if: (1) JDBC driver is on classpath, AND (2) no `DataSource` bean is already defined. You can override any auto-configured bean by defining your own.

**Key insight:** This is why `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` is the golden rule for library starters — never force a bean on the user.
problems.hint1: Maintain a Set<String> of defined bean types. 'MissingBean' = type not in the set.
problems.hint2: Check properties Map before creating the bean: if required property is absent, skip.
problems.hint3: User-defined beans take priority: register them before running auto-configuration.
problems.testCases:
Skipped DataSource auto-config: missing property or bean exists
Auto-configured MailService
DataSource: UserDefinedDataSource
MailService: AutoMailService[host=smtp.example.com]

Auto-configured DataSource
Skipped MailService auto-config: missing property or bean exists
DataSource: AutoDataSource[url=jdbc:h2:mem:test]
MailService: NOT_CREATED
problems.sampleInput: Two scenarios: user-defined DataSource + full props; then auto-only + missing mail.host
problems.sampleOutput: User bean takes precedence; missing property prevents auto-config

problems.title: Application Event Publisher
problems.description:
Implement Spring Boot's ApplicationEvent / ApplicationListener pattern to decouple side effects from business logic. When a user registers, fire a `UserRegisteredEvent` — email notification and audit log should react to it independently without the registration service knowing about them.

**Key insight:** Events decouple producers from consumers — adding a new side effect (e.g., Slack notification) means adding a new listener, not modifying UserService. In Spring Boot, `ApplicationEventPublisher.publishEvent()` fires events; `@EventListener` or `ApplicationListener<T>` consume them. Use `@Async` on listeners for non-blocking side effects.
problems.hint1: Define a UserRegisteredEvent record/class with userId and email fields.
problems.hint2: EventPublisher interface has publishEvent(Object). Two listeners: EmailListener and AuditListener.
problems.hint3: Simulate async with a flag; in real Spring Boot use @Async + @EnableAsync on a listener.
problems.testCases:
Registered user: usr-001
[Email] Sending welcome to alice@example.com
[Audit] User usr-001 registered at <timestamp>
problems.sampleOutput: Registration + two listener side effects fired independently
